passages.text: The Industrial Revolution was a period of profound social and economic change. It (17) --- the transition from hand production methods to machines. New chemical manufacturing and iron production processes were (18) ---, and the use of steam power increased significantly. (19) --- its benefits for productivity, the era also brought about harsh working conditions. Workers often faced long hours (20) --- very low wages. Therefore, many historians argue that while technology advanced, social equity (21) --- behind.

The human brain is an incredibly complex organ that functions as the control center of the body. It (22) --- of billions of neurons that communicate through electrical signals. Studies (23) --- that various regions of the brain are responsible for specific tasks such as memory and logic. (24) ---, the brain remains plastic, meaning it can reorganize itself in response to new experiences. This adaptability is (25) --- for learning and recovery from injury. Experts continue to investigate (26) --- the brain processes abstract concepts.

The Anthropocene is a proposed geological epoch dating from the commencement of significant human impact on Earth's geology and ecosystems. Although the term was popularized in 2000 by Paul Crutzen, the debate regarding its exact starting point continues. Some scientists argue it began with the Industrial Revolution in the late 18th century, while others point to the 'Great Acceleration' around 1950, characterized by a surge in plastic use and nuclear fallout. This new epoch signifies that human activity has become a dominant force in shaping the planet's physical and biological systems, potentially leading to irreversible changes in the climate and biodiversity.

The placebo effect remains one of the most intriguing phenomena in modern medicine. It occurs when a patient experiences a perceived or actual improvement in their condition after receiving a treatment that has no therapeutic value. Neuroscientific research suggests that the expectation of healing triggers the brain to release endorphins and dopamine. However, the ethical implications of using placebos in clinical practice are highly debated. While they can provide relief without side effects, the deceptive nature of the practice challenges the principle of informed consent, which is fundamental to the patient-doctor relationship.

The Library of Alexandria was one of the largest and most significant libraries of the ancient world. Established during the Hellenistic period in Egypt, it served as a major center for scholarship and the preservation of knowledge. It is estimated that the library housed hundreds of thousands of papyrus scrolls, covering subjects from mathematics to literature. The destruction of the library is a subject of historical controversy, with various accounts blaming Julius Caesar, the Caliph Omar, or internal decline. Regardless of the cause, its loss represented a catastrophic blow to the intellectual heritage of antiquity.

Bioluminescence is the production and emission of light by living organisms. It is a form of chemiluminescence that occurs primarily in marine vertebrates and invertebrates, as well as in some fungi and insects. The chemical reaction involves a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase. Organisms use bioluminescence for a variety of purposes, including luring prey, deterring predators, and attracting mates. In the deep sea, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence is the primary source of light, creating a unique and complex ecosystem driven by biological signals.

The Great Reset is a proposal by the World Economic Forum (WEF) to rebuild the economy sustainably following the COVID-19 pandemic. It suggests that the global crisis provided a unique opportunity to rethink traditional capitalist structures and address systemic inequalities. Critics, however, view the proposal with skepticism, fearing it grants too much power to unelected international bodies and multinational corporations. Proponents argue that without such a coordinated global effort, challenges like climate change and social instability will become unmanageable in the near future.

options.A: So, you mean I should eat more frequently?

options.D: When exactly did the Industrial Revolution end then?
options.E: Do you believe the 18th century was the peak of this change?

options.B: Although many people think vitamin C stops colds, it actually only makes the symptoms last a little less time.

options.D: There is no evidence at all that vitamin C has any impact on the common cold, contrary to popular belief.

options.C: The environmental damage was so bad that even strict regulations could not have prevented it.
options.D: The government is currently implementing strict regulations to reverse the environmental damage.

questions.question: It is not so much the lack of resources as it is the mismanagement of them that causes poverty in the region.

options.A: Poverty in the region is caused both by a total lack of resources and by their poor management.
options.B: If the region had more resources, the mismanagement would not lead to such high levels of poverty.
options.C: The primary cause of poverty in the area is how resources are handled, rather than a shortage of the resources themselves.
options.D: Mismanagement is a secondary issue; the real problem in the region is the absolute scarcity of resources.
options.E: Poverty could be eliminated if the region stopped relying on its limited resources altogether.

questions.question: The concept of 'emotional intelligence' (EQ) gained popularity in the 1990s. It refers to the ability to identify and manage one's own emotions, as well as the emotions of others. ---. For instance, people with high EQ are often better at leadership and conflict resolution, as they can navigate social complexities with greater ease.

options.A: However, traditional IQ is still the only valid measure of a person's future success.
options.B: Many psychologists argue that EQ is an innate trait that cannot be developed later in life.
options.C: This skill set is increasingly recognized as a vital component of professional and personal success.
options.D: The term was first coined by researchers who wanted to ignore the importance of cognitive ability.
options.E: Emotional intelligence is primarily concerned with how much a person can memorize.

questions.question: Renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar power, are essential for reducing carbon emissions. However, they face a significant challenge: intermittency. ---. To address this, scientists are developing advanced battery technologies to store energy for use when production is low.

options.A: Wind turbines are much more expensive to maintain than solar panels.
options.B: This means they do not produce power consistently, as the sun doesn't always shine and the wind doesn't always blow.
options.C: Fossil fuels remain the most reliable source of energy for the foreseeable future.
options.D: The global demand for electricity is expected to double by the year 2050.
options.E: Most countries have already transitioned entirely to green energy sources.

questions.question: Urbanization is a global trend that involves the migration of people from rural areas to cities. While it offers opportunities for economic growth and better access to services, it also puts immense pressure on infrastructure. ---. Without proper planning, these issues can lead to a decline in the quality of urban life.

options.A: Cities are becoming the primary hubs for technological innovation and cultural exchange.
options.B: Rural life is often perceived as being more peaceful and connected to nature.
options.C: This includes shortages in housing, increased traffic congestion, and strained public utilities.
options.D: Many people choose to move back to the countryside after living in a city for several years.
options.E: Governments should encourage more people to leave cities to balance the population.

questions.question: The Gothic novel emerged in the late 18th century as a genre characterized by mystery, horror, and the supernatural. It often featured gloomy settings like crumbling castles and dark forests. ---. This shift allowed the genre to explore the anxieties and hidden desires of the human mind.

options.A: The most famous Gothic novel is undoubtedly Mary Shelley's Frankenstein.
options.B: Over time, the focus shifted from external monsters to the internal psychological states of the characters.
options.C: Modern horror movies have very little in common with these early literary works.
options.D: Architectural styles in the 18th century were heavily influenced by classical Greek designs.
options.E: The genre was quickly suppressed by religious authorities who feared its influence.

questions.question: (I) Quantum computing utilizes the principles of quantum mechanics to process information in ways that classical computers cannot. (II) Unlike traditional bits, which are either 0 or 1, quantum bits or 'qubits' can exist in multiple states simultaneously. (III) This allows quantum computers to solve complex calculations much faster than the most powerful supercomputers today. (IV) Many people still prefer using laptops for their daily office tasks because they are portable. (V) However, building a stable quantum computer remains a significant engineering challenge due to the fragility of qubits.

options.A: I
options.B: II
options.C: III
options.D: IV
options.E: V

questions.question: (I) Ocean acidification is a direct consequence of increased carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere. (II) When the ocean absorbs CO2, the water becomes more acidic, which can harm marine life. (III) Corals and shellfish are particularly vulnerable as they cannot form their shells in acidic environments. (IV) Many coastal communities rely on fishing as their primary source of income and food. (V) If these ecosystems collapse, it will have devastating effects on global marine biodiversity.

questions.question: (I) Standardized testing has long been a staple of the education system in many countries. (II) Proponents argue that it provides an objective measure of student achievement and teacher effectiveness. (III) Most students find that studying in groups is more helpful than studying alone. (IV) However, critics claim that these tests narrow the curriculum and place too much pressure on young learners. (V) They suggest that a more holistic approach to assessment would better reflect a student's true potential.

questions.question: (I) Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. (II) This process allows the neurons in the brain to compensate for injury and disease. (III) It also enables the brain to adjust its activities in response to new situations or changes in the environment. (IV) Some people believe that eating walnuts can improve memory because they look like little brains. (V) This discovery has revolutionized our understanding of how the brain can recover and adapt even in adulthood.
